---
title: "Analysis of Financial and Psychological Distress in the Australian Workforce"
subtitle: "A repeated cross-sectional analysis of ITW survey waves, workplace conditions and economic context, 2024-2025"
author: "Dev Gur"
date: "8 June 2026"

format:
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    colorlinks: true
    geometry:
      - margin=1in
    fontsize: 10pt
    fig-pos: "H"
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        \usepackage{array}
        \usepackage{ragged2e}
        \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.25}
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    code-fold: true

execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

```{r setup}
# Load packages used for data cleaning, visualisation, modelling and reporting
library(tidyverse)
library(haven)
library(janitor)
library(forcats)
library(scales)
library(readxl)
library(lubridate)
library(broom)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)

options(dplyr.summarise.inform = FALSE)

# Reusable table styling function for consistent PDF/HTML output
style_report_table <- function(x, caption = NULL, font_size = 8, widths = NULL) {
  
  names(x) <- stringr::str_replace_all(names(x), "_", " ")
  
  tab <- kable(
    x,
    caption = caption,
    booktabs = TRUE,
    escape = TRUE,
    linesep = "",
    align = "l"
  )
  
  if (knitr::is_latex_output()) {
    tab <- tab |>
      kable_styling(
        latex_options = c("hold_position"),
        font_size = font_size,
        full_width = FALSE,
        position = "center"
      )
    
    if (!is.null(widths)) {
      for (i in seq_along(widths)) {
        tab <- tab |>
          column_spec(
            i,
            width = widths[i],
            latex_valign = "m"
          )
      }
    }
  } else {
    tab <- tab |>
      kable_styling(
        full_width = FALSE,
        position = "center"
      )
  }
  
  tab
}
```

# Abstract

Workplace mental health reflects a complex interaction between individual psychological symptoms, workplace conditions and broader economic pressures. As Australian workers continue to experience changing organisational and financial conditions, there is value in monitoring whether workforce wellbeing indicators move in ways that are meaningful for planning, prevention and support. This is especially important for organisations such as SuperFriend, whose work focuses on supporting mentally healthy workplaces and helping employers understand where workplace action may be needed.

This report presents a repeated cross-sectional analysis of SuperFriend's Indicators of a Thriving Workplace survey waves from 2024 and 2025. The project objective was to develop a repeatable approach that helps SuperFriend distinguish meaningful workforce wellbeing signals from normal quarter-to-quarter variation, and translate those signals into practical workplace action. In this report, a meaningful signal is interpreted as a pattern that is visible in the data, supported by statistical checks where appropriate, and useful for decision-making. This definition deliberately combines statistical and practical meaning. A pattern may be statistically interesting, but for SuperFriend it is most valuable when it can help identify when support may be needed, which groups may require attention, and what workplace or economic context should be considered.

The analysis is organised around three practical questions. First, **When does workforce wellbeing risk appear to increase?** Second, **Who appears to be more affected?** Third, **What workplace and economic context helps explain the observed patterns?** The report examines psychological distress, burnout, SuperFriend workplace domains, psychosocial hazard management, financial distress, age groups, occupation type, the ABS Living Cost Index and the RBA cash rate.

The methods combine descriptive visualisation, 95% confidence intervals, independent quarter comparisons with Bonferroni correction, linear trend models, chi-square tests, proportion trend tests and correlation analysis. These methods were selected because the data is repeated cross-sectional rather than longitudinal. The analysis therefore focuses on workforce-level signals across survey waves, rather than individual-level change.

The results suggest that psychological distress appears elevated around Q2, some groups show higher or more variable distress than others, and financial distress is positively associated with psychological distress. Workplace domains and psychosocial hazard management scores provide practical workplace levers, while economic indicators such as living costs and interest rates provide useful context. Because the ITW data is repeated cross-sectional rather than longitudinal, the findings are interpreted as workforce-level signals and associations, not individual-level change or causal proof.

# Introduction

## Project context

SuperFriend works to support mentally healthy workplaces in Australia. In this report, ITW refers to SuperFriend’s *Indicators of a Thriving Workplace* survey. ITW is a workforce survey that collects information about workers’ wellbeing, workplace experience, psychosocial conditions and related pressures.

The ITW survey has been implemented annually by SuperFriend for the past 11 years to monitor workplace mental health and related workplace conditions across the Australian workforce. Since 2024, SuperFriend has collected ITW data quarterly through ORU, a social research organisation. This quarterly collection allows workplace mental health indicators to be tracked across the year and helps identify possible seasonal patterns. ITW is one of Australia’s major workplace mental health surveys. It captures information across all 19 ABS-defined industries and is designed to provide a national picture of workplace mental health, psychosocial conditions and related worker experiences. This makes it useful for identifying where risk may be concentrated and where workplace action may be most needed. The ITW survey and SuperFriend Domains are used in this report as the project’s main workplace mental health framework (SuperFriend, 2025).

The project was motivated by a practical problem. SuperFriend needed a repeatable way to determine whether changes in ITW measures over time were meaningful and useful for action, rather than simply normal movement between survey waves. This is important because survey estimates naturally fluctuate. A graph may show movement, but an organisation needs a stronger approach for deciding whether that movement is large enough, consistent enough or relevant enough to support planning.

The project therefore sits at the intersection of data analysis and workplace decision-making. From a statistical perspective, the main challenge is that the data is repeated cross-sectional rather than longitudinal. This means the same workers are not necessarily followed from one quarter to the next. From a business analytics perspective, the challenge is to turn repeated survey data into information that is clear enough to guide organisational action.

A longitudinal design was not used because it would require the same participants to respond repeatedly over time. In this project context, that would place a high commitment on participants and would likely lead to substantial attrition at each time point. Instead, SuperFriend uses repeated cross-sectional survey waves, with the annual dataset designed to be representative of the national workforce across key characteristics such as state, gender, age group and the 19 ABS industry categories.

Workplace mental health is not shaped by one factor alone. Psychological distress may reflect personal, workplace and economic pressures. Burnout may reflect longer-term accumulated strain. Workplace domains such as leadership, safety, connectedness, work design and capability provide information about organisational conditions. Psychosocial hazard management scores provide insight into how well workplace risks are being managed. Financial distress and external economic indicators provide broader context about the cost-of-living environment in which workers are living.

Bringing these measures together makes the project more complex, but also more useful, because it allows workforce wellbeing to be interpreted as a system rather than a single score. For SuperFriend, the value of the analysis is not only in identifying whether a number has changed, but in understanding whether the change can help inform prevention, support, advocacy or future monitoring.

This report therefore combines exploratory data analysis, visualisation, statistical testing and contextual interpretation. The intention is not simply to produce charts. The intention is to build a repeatable workflow that SuperFriend could reuse when new ITW waves become available. This workflow is designed to support a practical question: **What signals are emerging from the data, and how can they inform targeted workplace action?**

## Project objective

The objective of this project was to develop a repeatable cross-sectional trend approach that SuperFriend can apply to future ITW survey waves.

The approach aimed to:

- Clean and combine repeated ITW survey waves;
- Calculate consistent mental health, workplace and financial measures;
- Compare results across quarters, years and selected groups;
- Use statistical checks to avoid overclaiming visual patterns;
- Integrate financial and economic context where relevant;
- Communicate findings in a way that supports practical workplace action.

This objective reflects both an analytical and practical contribution. Analytically, the project provides a structured way to work with repeated cross-sectional survey data. Practically, it helps SuperFriend move from observing separate survey results to monitoring repeated workforce-level signals over time.

## Significance of the project

The significance of this project comes from the need to make repeated survey data useful for decision-making. Organisations often collect survey data over time, but without a repeatable methodology, it can be difficult to know whether apparent changes are important or simply normal variation. This is especially important in mental health and wellbeing analysis, where overclaiming can lead to misleading conclusions, while under-interpreting can cause important signals to be missed.

The project contributes a practical framework for repeated ITW wave analysis. The framework is not a single predictive model. Instead, it combines data preparation, score construction, visualisation, statistical checks and cautious interpretation. This is appropriate for the project because the core problem is not only prediction, but monitoring and interpretation. The analysis needed to help SuperFriend understand when signals appear, who may be more affected, and what context may help explain the patterns.

The five SuperFriend Domains provide an evidence-based framework for interpreting workplace mental health conditions. They help translate survey results into practical workplace areas such as Connectedness, Safety, Leadership, Work Design and Capability. This is important because workplace mental health action should not only focus on reducing harm, but also on strengthening protective workplace conditions.

A novel feature of the project is the combination of workplace mental health indicators with broader financial and economic context. Psychological distress and burnout are analysed alongside workplace domains, psychosocial hazard management, financial distress, living costs and interest rates. This helps position workforce wellbeing as both a workplace and economic issue. It also allows the report to move beyond a narrow interpretation of mental health and consider the wider environment in which workers experience pressure.

## Report storyline

To improve cohesion, this report is structured around three applied questions.

```{r storyline-table}
storyline <- tibble(
  Question = c(
    "When does risk increase?",
    "Who appears more affected?",
    "What helps explain or contextualise risk?"
  ),
  Purpose = c(
    "Identify timing of elevated workforce distress",
    "Identify whether risk differs by industry, age or occupation",
    "Connect distress patterns with workplace and economic context"
  ),
  `Main analyses` = c(
    "K6, BAT-12, quarterly means, confidence intervals, trend models",
    "Retail vs Transport, age groups, blue-collar vs white-collar workers",
    "Domains, psychosocial hazard management, financial distress, ABS LCI, RBA cash rate"
  )
)

style_report_table(
  storyline,
  caption = "Report storyline linking findings to the project objective",
  font_size = 8,
  widths = c("3cm", "5.2cm", "6cm")
)
```

This structure helps link every analysis back to the same project goal: helping SuperFriend identify workforce wellbeing signals that can inform targeted action. It also avoids presenting the results as a long list of unrelated charts. Each result is interpreted in relation to one of the three applied questions.

# Data and measures

## Data sources

The main data source was SuperFriend's ITW (Indicators of a Thriving Workplace) survey data for 2024 and 2025. Each year included quarterly survey waves. The underlying data consists of individual worker survey responses. The analysis starts from individual records, but many results are summarised by quarter, year, industry, age group or occupation group.

The analysis also incorporated two external economic indicators:

- ABS Employee households Living Cost Index;
- RBA cash rate.

These external indicators were aligned with the ITW survey data at the quarterly level. They are used as economic context, not as direct causal predictors. Because the ITW data contains survey responses, raw data files are not included publicly in the Git repository unless sharing has been approved.

## Repeated cross-sectional design

The ITW data is repeated cross-sectional, not longitudinal. This means that each quarterly wave contains worker responses, but the same workers are not necessarily followed over time. A longitudinal design was not used because it would require the same participants to respond repeatedly across time. For this project context, that would place a high commitment on participants and would likely lead to substantial attrition at each time point. Instead, SuperFriend uses repeated cross-sectional survey waves, with each annual dataset designed to remain representative of the national workforce across key characteristics such as state, gender, age group and industry.

This distinction is central to the report. A longitudinal design would allow analysis of how the same workers changed across time. In contrast, a repeated cross-sectional design allows analysis of how workforce-level estimates differ across waves. This is still useful for organisational monitoring, but the interpretation is different. The results can describe population-level or workforce-level patterns across quarters, but they cannot prove that the same individual workers became more or less distressed over time.

For this reason, line plots in the report connect quarterly group means for visual clarity only. They should not be interpreted as individual level trajectories. Because the ITW data is repeated cross-sectional, the report uses cautious language when interpreting results. Terms such as “appears”, “suggests”, “is associated with”, “may indicate” and “provides context” are used to emphasise that the project identifies workforce-level patterns and relationships, rather than causal effects or individual-level change.

## Key measures

```{r measures-table}
measures <- tibble(
  Measure = c(
    "K6 psychological distress: 0–24",
    "K6 >= 13",
    "BAT-12 burnout: 1–5 average score",
    "SuperFriend domains: 0–100",
    "Psychosocial hazard management: 0–100",
    "Financial distress: 1–10",
    "ABS Employee households LCI",
    "RBA cash rate"
  ),
  Meaning = c(
    "Psychological distress score",
    "Moderate-to-severe psychological distress threshold",
    "Burnout score",
    "Workplace conditions that support thriving",
    "Selected indicators of how psychosocial hazards are managed",
    "Worker financial pressure",
    "Cost-of-living context for working households",
    "Interest-rate context"
  ),
  Interpretation = c(
    "Higher = greater psychological distress",
    "Higher proportion = larger high-risk group",
    "Higher = greater burnout",
    "Higher = stronger workplace performance / better management",
    "Higher = better management of psychosocial hazards",
    "Higher = greater financial distress",
    "External economic context, not causal proof",
    "External economic context, not causal proof"
  )
)

style_report_table(
  measures,
  caption = "Main measures used in the report",
  font_size = 8,
  widths = c("3cm", "5.2cm", "6cm")
)
```

The key measures were selected because together they represent different parts of workforce wellbeing. K6 provides a measure of psychological distress. BAT-12 provides a measure of burnout. The SuperFriend domain scores provide information about workplace conditions that may support or weaken worker wellbeing. Psychosocial hazard management scores indicate how well selected workplace risks appear to be managed. Financial distress and external economic indicators provide context about household and macroeconomic pressure.

This combination of measures is important because mental health in the workforce cannot be understood through one variable alone. For example, a rise in psychological distress may be more meaningful if it occurs alongside weaker workplace domain scores or greater financial distress. Similarly, stable burnout does not necessarily mean there is no wellbeing risk; it may suggest that burnout is slower-moving than psychological distress. The report therefore interprets these measures together rather than treating each graph as a separate result. The K6 measure is widely used as a short screening scale for non-specific psychological distress (Kessler et al., 2002). The BAT-12 measure is used in this report as a burnout indicator, with higher scores interpreted as greater burnout (SuperFriend, 2025).

# Data preparation

The data preparation stage combined the 2024 and 2025 ITW survey files, harmonised key variable names, created quarter and year variables, and calculated the core mental health, workplace and financial distress measures used in the report. This step was important because all later comparisons rely on consistent scoring and consistent quarterly labels.

```{r data-prep}
# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 1. Read ITW files
# ------------------------------------------------------------

sav_files <- list.files(
  path = "data",
  pattern = "\\.sav$",
  recursive = TRUE,
  full.names = TRUE,
  ignore.case = TRUE
)

file_2024 <- sav_files[str_detect(basename(sav_files), regex("2024", ignore_case = TRUE))][1]
file_2025 <- sav_files[str_detect(basename(sav_files), regex("2025", ignore_case = TRUE))][1]

if (is.na(file_2024) | is.na(file_2025)) {
  stop("Could not find both 2024 and 2025 .sav files in the data folder.")
}

itw_2024 <- read_sav(file_2024) |>
  haven::zap_labels() |>
  haven::zap_label() |>
  clean_names() |>
  mutate(data_year = 2024)

itw_2025 <- read_sav(file_2025) |>
  haven::zap_labels() |>
  haven::zap_label() |>
  clean_names() |>
  mutate(data_year = 2025)

itw_all <- bind_rows(itw_2024, itw_2025)

# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 2. Helper functions
# ------------------------------------------------------------

# Select the first available numeric variable from a list of possible names.
# This makes the code more robust if variable names differ slightly across files.
pick_num <- function(df, possible_names) {
  selected_name <- possible_names[possible_names %in% names(df)][1]
  
  if (is.na(selected_name)) {
    return(rep(NA_real_, nrow(df)))
  }
  
  as.numeric(df[[selected_name]])
}

# Select the first available labelled/categorical variable from possible names.
# This is useful for variables such as industry or demographic groups.
pick_factor <- function(df, possible_names) {
  selected_name <- possible_names[possible_names %in% names(df)][1]
  
  if (is.na(selected_name)) {
    return(rep(NA_character_, nrow(df)))
  }
  
  as.character(as_factor(df[[selected_name]]))
}

# Convert SuperFriend domain items to a 0-100 score.
# The formula assumes original items are measured on a 1-5 scale.
# Higher scores indicate stronger workplace performance or better management.
score_domain <- function(df, item_names) {
  item_names <- intersect(item_names, names(df))
  
  if (length(item_names) == 0) {
    return(rep(NA_real_, nrow(df)))
  }
  
  m <- rowMeans(as.data.frame(lapply(df[item_names], as.numeric)), na.rm = TRUE)
  s <- (m - 1) * 25
  s[is.nan(m)] <- NA_real_
  s
}

# Convert selected psychosocial hazard management items to a 0-100 score.
# The same 1-5 to 0-100 transformation is used for consistency with domain scores.
# Higher scores indicate better management of psychosocial hazards.
score_hazard <- function(df, item_names) {
  item_names <- intersect(item_names, names(df))
  
  if (length(item_names) == 0) {
    return(rep(NA_real_, nrow(df)))
  }
  
  m <- rowMeans(as.data.frame(lapply(df[item_names], as.numeric)), na.rm = TRUE)
  s <- (m - 1) * 25
  s[is.nan(m)] <- NA_real_
  s
}

# Calculate group means and approximate 95% confidence intervals.
# These summaries are used in plots to show both average scores and uncertainty.
mean_ci <- function(df, variable, group_vars) {
  df |>
    group_by(across(all_of(group_vars))) |>
    summarise(
      mean = mean(.data[[variable]], na.rm = TRUE),
      sd = sd(.data[[variable]], na.rm = TRUE),
      n = sum(!is.na(.data[[variable]])),
      se = sd / sqrt(n),
      lower = mean - 1.96 * se,
      upper = mean + 1.96 * se,
      .groups = "drop"
    )
}

# Safely calculate correlations only when there are enough complete observations.
# This avoids errors when a variable has too many missing values.
safe_cor <- function(x, y) {
  complete <- complete.cases(x, y)
  if (sum(complete) < 3) {
    return(list(estimate = NA_real_, p.value = NA_real_))
  }
  cor.test(x[complete], y[complete])
}

# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 3. Core variables
# ------------------------------------------------------------

# Extract year and wave information before creating standard quarter labels.
# These labels allow 2024 and 2025 survey waves to be compared consistently.
year_raw <- pick_num(itw_all, c("year"))
wave_raw <- pick_num(itw_all, c("wave", "quarter", "timepoint"))

itw_all <- itw_all |>
  mutate(
    year = if_else(is.na(year_raw), as.integer(data_year), as.integer(year_raw)),
    wave = as.integer(wave_raw),
    quarter = factor(
      paste0("Q", wave),
      levels = c("Q1", "Q2", "Q3", "Q4"),
      ordered = TRUE
    )
  )

quarter_order <- c(
  "2024 Q1", "2024 Q2", "2024 Q3", "2024 Q4",
  "2025 Q1", "2025 Q2", "2025 Q3", "2025 Q4"
)

itw_all <- itw_all |>
  mutate(
    quarter_full = factor(
      paste(year, quarter),
      levels = quarter_order,
      ordered = TRUE
    ),
    time_index = as.numeric(quarter_full),
    
    k6_score = pick_num(cur_data(), c("k6score", "k6_score", "k6_total")),
    bat12_total = pick_num(cur_data(), c("bat12_total", "bat_12_total", "burnout")),
    financial_distress = pick_num(cur_data(), c("f10_1", "financial_distress")),
    
    age_code = pick_num(cur_data(), c("age")),
    age_group = case_when(
      age_code == 1 ~ "18 to 24 years",
      age_code == 2 ~ "25 to 34 years",
      age_code == 3 ~ "35 to 44 years",
      age_code == 4 ~ "45 to 54 years",
      age_code == 5 ~ "55 to 64 years",
      age_code == 6 ~ "65+ years",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    age_group = factor(
      age_group,
      levels = c(
        "18 to 24 years",
        "25 to 34 years",
        "35 to 44 years",
        "45 to 54 years",
        "55 to 64 years",
        "65+ years"
      ),
      ordered = TRUE
    ),
    
    industry_number = pick_num(cur_data(), c("a6", "industry")),
    industry_label = if ("a6" %in% names(itw_all)) {
      as.character(as_factor(a6))
    } else {
      as.character(industry_number)
    },
    
    industry_focus = case_when(
      industry_number == 7 ~ "Retail",
      industry_number == 9 ~ "Transport",
      str_detect(industry_label, regex("retail", ignore_case = TRUE)) ~ "Retail",
      str_detect(industry_label, regex("transport", ignore_case = TRUE)) ~ "Transport",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    
    worker_code = pick_num(cur_data(), c("a18")),
    worker_type = case_when(
      worker_code == 2 ~ "Blue collar",
      worker_code == 1 ~ "White collar",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    
    k6_high = if_else(k6_score >= 13, 1, 0, missing = NA_real_)
  )

# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 4. SuperFriend domain scores
# ------------------------------------------------------------

# Define item groups used to construct the five SuperFriend workplace domains.
# Each domain score is calculated from the relevant survey items.
connectedness_items <- paste0("b1_", 1:10)
leadership_items <- paste0("b2_", 1:10)

safety_items <- c(
  "b3x1_1",
  paste0("b3x2_", 1:3),
  paste0("b3x3_", 1:3),
  paste0("b3x4_", 1:3)
)

work_design_items <- c(
  paste0("b4x1_", 1:3),
  paste0("b4x2_", 1:4),
  paste0("b4x3_", 1:3)
)

capability_items <- paste0("b5_", 1:7)

itw_all <- itw_all |>
  mutate(
    Connectedness = score_domain(cur_data(), connectedness_items),
    Leadership = score_domain(cur_data(), leadership_items),
    Safety = score_domain(cur_data(), safety_items),
    Work_Design = score_domain(cur_data(), work_design_items),
    Capability = score_domain(cur_data(), capability_items)
  )

# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 5. Psychosocial hazard management scores
# ------------------------------------------------------------

# Define item groups used to create selected psychosocial hazard management scores.
# These scores help connect wellbeing outcomes with workplace conditions.
management_support_items <- c("b2_1", "b2_2", "b2_3", "b2_4", "b2_5")
recognition_items <- c("b2_6", "b2_7", "b2_8", "b2_9", "b2_10")
workload_items <- c("b4x1_1", "b4x1_2", "b4x1_3")

itw_all <- itw_all |>
  mutate(
    Management_Support = score_hazard(cur_data(), management_support_items),
    Recognition = score_hazard(cur_data(), recognition_items),
    Workload = score_hazard(cur_data(), workload_items)
  )

# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 6. Summary datasets
# ------------------------------------------------------------

# Create summary datasets used for figures and statistical interpretation.
# These summaries keep the plotting code simpler and ensure consistent calculations.
k6_national <- mean_ci(itw_all, "k6_score", c("year", "quarter"))

burnout_national <- mean_ci(itw_all, "bat12_total", c("year", "quarter"))

industry_k6 <- itw_all |>
  filter(!is.na(industry_focus)) |>
  mean_ci("k6_score", c("industry_focus", "year", "quarter"))

industry_burnout <- itw_all |>
  filter(!is.na(industry_focus)) |>
  mean_ci("bat12_total", c("industry_focus", "year", "quarter"))

domain_summary <- itw_all |>
  select(year, quarter, quarter_full, Connectedness, Leadership, Safety, Work_Design, Capability) |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(Connectedness, Leadership, Safety, Work_Design, Capability),
    names_to = "domain",
    values_to = "score"
  ) |>
  mutate(domain = str_replace_all(domain, "_", " ")) |>
  group_by(year, quarter, domain) |>
  summarise(
    mean_score = mean(score, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd = sd(score, na.rm = TRUE),
    n = sum(!is.na(score)),
    se = sd / sqrt(n),
    lower = mean_score - 1.96 * se,
    upper = mean_score + 1.96 * se,
    .groups = "drop"
  )

hazard_summary <- itw_all |>
  select(year, quarter, quarter_full, Management_Support, Recognition, Workload) |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(Management_Support, Recognition, Workload),
    names_to = "hazard",
    values_to = "score"
  ) |>
  mutate(hazard = str_replace_all(hazard, "_", " ")) |>
  group_by(year, quarter, hazard) |>
  summarise(
    mean_score = mean(score, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd = sd(score, na.rm = TRUE),
    n = sum(!is.na(score)),
    se = sd / sqrt(n),
    lower = mean_score - 1.96 * se,
    upper = mean_score + 1.96 * se,
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# Create quarterly financial distress summaries and combine them with mean K6.
# This dataset supports the economic-context section of the report.
phase2_financial <- itw_all |>
  group_by(quarter_full) |>
  summarise(
    mean_f10 = mean(financial_distress, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_k6 = mean(k6_score, na.rm = TRUE),
    high_f10_pct = mean(financial_distress >= 8, na.rm = TRUE) * 100,
    n = sum(!is.na(financial_distress)),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# Summarise financial distress by age group and quarter for the heatmap.
# This helps identify whether financial pressure is concentrated in younger or older groups.
age_financial <- itw_all |>
  filter(!is.na(age_group), !is.na(financial_distress)) |>
  group_by(quarter_full, age_group) |>
  summarise(
    mean_f10 = mean(financial_distress, na.rm = TRUE),
    n = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# Summarise financial distress by occupation type and quarter.
# This helps compare blue-collar and white-collar worker patterns.
worker_financial <- itw_all |>
  filter(!is.na(worker_type), !is.na(financial_distress)) |>
  group_by(quarter_full, worker_type) |>
  summarise(
    mean_f10 = mean(financial_distress, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_k6 = mean(k6_score, na.rm = TRUE),
    n = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# Use consistent colours for 2024 and 2025 across all figures.
year_cols <- c("2024" = "#1E40FF", "2025" = "#E31A1C")
```

The final prepared dataset contains standardised quarter labels, mental health measures, financial distress variables, workplace domain scores, psychosocial hazard management scores and selected grouping variables. These prepared variables are used throughout the results sections.

The main data-processing contribution was the creation of a consistent analysis-ready dataset from repeated survey files. This required harmonising variable names, checking that key measures were comparable across years, creating quarter identifiers, calculating derived scores and preparing grouped summaries for visualisation and statistical testing. This work is important because repeated wave analysis depends on consistency. If quarters, scores or group labels are not created consistently, later comparisons may reflect data-processing differences rather than meaningful workforce patterns.

# Methodology

## Exploratory data analysis and visualisation

After the ITW files were cleaned, harmonised and converted into an analysis-ready dataset, exploratory data analysis (EDA) was used as the first analytical stage of the project. The purpose of EDA was to understand the structure of the prepared data, identify early patterns across survey waves, compare key workforce groups, and decide which variables required further statistical checking.

In this report, data preparation and EDA are treated as separate stages. Data preparation involved combining the 2024 and 2025 files, standardising quarter labels, constructing key measures, and creating analysis variables. EDA then used the prepared dataset to explore patterns in psychological distress, burnout, financial distress, workplace domains and psychosocial hazard management.

Visualisation was central to the EDA stage because the project aimed to identify workforce-level signals over time. Different graph types were used for different analytical questions. Line charts with confidence intervals were used to compare quarterly averages, violin plots were used to show the spread of psychological distress and identify the high-distress tail, heatmaps were used to compare financial distress across groups and quarters, and scatterplots were used to examine the association between financial distress and psychological distress.

The visual design was chosen to support interpretation rather than decoration. For example, quarterly line plots made it easier to compare 2024 and 2025 patterns, while confidence intervals helped show uncertainty around the estimates. Violin plots were useful because average K6 scores alone could hide whether a smaller group of workers was experiencing much higher distress. Heatmaps were useful for identifying whether financial distress was concentrated in particular age groups or time periods. Scatterplots helped connect psychological distress outcomes with financial distress and broader economic context.

Because the ITW data is repeated cross-sectional, the visualisations were interpreted carefully. The lines in the time-series style plots connect quarterly group means for visual clarity only. They do not show the same individual workers being followed over time. This is a key methodological caveat. Therefore, the report avoids language such as “workers became more distressed” and instead uses wording such as “average distress appears higher in this wave” or “the workforce-level estimate appears elevated.”

Overall, EDA helped shape the rest of the analysis. It identified the main patterns to investigate, guided which subgroup comparisons were most useful, and provided the basis for later statistical checks such as confidence intervals, independent quarter comparisons, trend models and threshold tests.


## Statistical testing

Statistical testing was used to support the visual analysis and reduce the risk of overclaiming. The tests were selected to match the type of question being asked.

```{r statistical-tests}
# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 1. Independent quarter comparisons with Bonferroni correction
# ------------------------------------------------------------

# Function to compare quarterly means for one outcome within one year.
# The comparisons are independent because the same workers are not followed over time.
run_quarter_comparisons <- function(df, outcome) {
 
 # Keep only the quarter and outcome variable, then remove missing values. 
  df2 <- df |>
    select(quarter, all_of(outcome)) |>
    drop_na()
  
  # If fewer than two quarters are available, no comparison can be made.
  if (n_distinct(df2$quarter) < 2) {
    return(tibble())
  }
  
  # Calculate quarter means so the direction and size of pairwise differences can be reported
  means_tbl <- df2 |>
    group_by(quarter) |>
    summarise(mean_value = mean(.data[[outcome]], na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")
  
  # Run pairwise t-tests between quarters.
  # p-values are adjusted later within each year-outcome group using Bonferroni correction.
  pairwise.t.test(
    x = df2[[outcome]],
    g = df2$quarter,
    p.adjust.method = "none"
  ) |>
    broom::tidy() |>
    rowwise() |>
     # Estimate is calculated as the difference between quarter means.
    mutate(
      estimate = means_tbl$mean_value[means_tbl$quarter == group2] -
        means_tbl$mean_value[means_tbl$quarter == group1],
      outcome = outcome
    ) |>
    ungroup()
}

# Outcomes tested across quarters.
# These include mental health outcomes, workplace domains and psychosocial hazard measures.
outcomes <- c(
  "k6_score", "bat12_total",
  "Connectedness", "Leadership", "Safety", "Work_Design", "Capability",
  "Management_Support", "Recognition", "Workload"
)

# Apply the quarter comparison function separately for each year and each outcome.
quarter_tests <- bind_rows(
  lapply(c(2024, 2025), function(yr) {
    bind_rows(
      lapply(outcomes, function(out) {
        run_quarter_comparisons(
          filter(itw_all, year == yr),
          out
        ) |>
          mutate(year = yr)
      })
    )
  })
) |>
  group_by(year, outcome) |>
   # Bonferroni correction reduces false positives from multiple quarter comparisons.
  mutate(
    p_adj = p.adjust(p.value, method = "bonferroni"),
    significant = p_adj < 0.05
  ) |>
  ungroup()

# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 2. Linear trend models across eight waves
# ------------------------------------------------------------

# Function to test whether an outcome shows a linear increase or decrease
# across the ordered 2024-2025 quarterly survey waves.
run_trend_model <- function(df, outcome) {
  lm(as.formula(paste(outcome, "~ time_index")), data = df) |>
    tidy() |>
    filter(term == "time_index") |>
    mutate(outcome = outcome)
}

# Run the trend model for each main outcome.
# These models are used as supporting evidence, not as causal models.
trend_results <- bind_rows(
  lapply(outcomes, function(out) {
    run_trend_model(itw_all, out)
  })
)

# ------------------------------------------------------------
# 3. K6 >= 13 threshold tests
# ------------------------------------------------------------

# Test whether the proportion of workers above the K6 >= 13 threshold
# differs across all survey waves at the national level.
k6_threshold_tests <- itw_all |>
  filter(!is.na(k6_high), !is.na(quarter_full)) |>
  summarise(
    chisq_p = chisq.test(table(quarter_full, k6_high))$p.value,
    trend_p = {
      tbl <- table(quarter_full, k6_high)
      if ("1" %in% colnames(tbl)) {
        prop.trend.test(tbl[, "1"], rowSums(tbl))$p.value
      } else {
        NA_real_
      }
    }
  )

# Repeat the K6 threshold tests separately for Retail and Transport.
# This checks whether the high-distress group behaves differently by industry.
industry_threshold_tests <- itw_all |>
  filter(!is.na(industry_focus), !is.na(k6_high), !is.na(quarter_full)) |>
  group_by(industry_focus) |>
  summarise(
    chisq_p = chisq.test(table(quarter_full, k6_high))$p.value,
    trend_p = {
      tbl <- table(quarter_full, k6_high)
      if ("1" %in% colnames(tbl)) {
        prop.trend.test(tbl[, "1"], rowSums(tbl))$p.value
      } else {
        NA_real_
      }
    },
    .groups = "drop"
  )
```

```{r test-summary-table}
test_summary <- tibble(
  Method = c(
    "Independent quarter comparisons",
    "Bonferroni correction",
    "Linear trend models",
    "Chi-square test",
    "Proportion trend test",
    "Pearson correlation"
  ),
  Purpose = c(
    "Compare mean scores between quarters",
    "Reduce false positives from many quarter comparisons",
    "Assess whether outcomes increased or decreased across waves",
    "Test whether the high-distress group changed across waves",
    "Test whether the high-distress proportion showed a directional pattern",
    "Explore associations between financial distress, psychological distress and economic indicators"
  ),
  Interpretation = c(
    "Used for quarter-level differences, not paired individual change",
    "Makes inference more conservative",
    "Tests direction of workforce-level change",
    "Used for distress >= 13 threshold analysis",
    "Used for ordered high-risk proportion analysis",
    "Association only, not causation"
  )
)

style_report_table(
  test_summary,
  caption = "Statistical tests used and why",
  font_size = 8,
  widths = c("3.4cm", "6cm", "5.2cm")
)
```

The quarter comparisons are described as independent comparisons because the same individuals were not followed over time. This is an important correction because paired tests would only be appropriate if the same participants were measured repeatedly. In this project, each wave is treated as a separate cross-sectional sample.

Bonferroni correction was used because multiple comparisons can increase the chance of false positive findings. Linear regression models were used to assess gradual workforce-level trends across waves. Chi-square and proportion trend tests were used for the K6 >= 13 threshold analysis. Correlations were used to examine relationships between financial distress, psychological distress and external economic indicators.

The statistical tests were not intended to replace visual interpretation. Instead, they were used as checks. Visualisations help identify patterns, while the tests help assess whether those patterns are likely to be more than ordinary quarter-to-quarter variation. This combination was useful because the project required both statistical caution and practical communication.

## Robustness and interpretation checks

Several checks were used to reduce the risk of over-interpreting visual patterns. First, 95% confidence intervals were included for key quarterly mean estimates to show uncertainty around the averages. Second, independent quarter comparisons were adjusted using Bonferroni correction to reduce the risk of false positive findings from multiple testing. Third, linear trend models were used alongside visualisations to assess whether patterns showed a directional workforce-level trend across waves. Finally, K6 >= 13 was analysed separately to check whether patterns were also visible in the higher-risk distress group, not only in average scores.

These checks do not remove the limitations of repeated cross-sectional data. However, they make the interpretation more cautious and help distinguish stronger workforce-level signals from normal quarter-to-quarter variation. The report therefore treats the findings as signals for monitoring and decision-making, rather than as definitive proof of cause and effect.

## Methodological contribution and skills developed

A key contribution of this project was the development of a repeatable workflow for analysing repeated ITW survey waves. The work required combining data cleaning, variable harmonisation, score construction, visualisation, statistical testing and cautious interpretation into one coherent process. This was methodologically important because the data was repeated cross-sectional rather than longitudinal, meaning that the analysis had to focus on workforce-level signals rather than individual-level change.

The project also involved several technical challenges. The 2024 and 2025 files needed to be harmonised so that variables were comparable across years. Workplace domain and psychosocial hazard scores had to be calculated consistently. External economic indicators such as the ABS Living Cost Index and RBA cash rate had to be aligned to quarterly survey waves. Another challenge was communicating the results clearly without overclaiming causation.

Through this work, I strengthened skills in data cleaning, survey data analysis, score construction, statistical testing, visualisation and reproducible reporting in R and Quarto. The main innovation of the project is not a single model, but the creation of a practical and reusable analytical framework that SuperFriend can apply to future ITW waves.

# Results: When does risk increase?

## Psychological distress across quarters

```{r fig-k6-national, fig.cap="Quarterly mean K6 psychological distress scores with 95% confidence intervals. Lines connect repeated cross-sectional group means for visual clarity only. Higher K6 scores indicate greater psychological distress", fig.width=7, fig.height=4.3}

# Plot quarterly mean K6 psychological distress scores.
# Error bars show 95% confidence intervals around each quarterly mean
ggplot(
  k6_national,
  aes(x = quarter, y = mean, colour = factor(year), group = year)
) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(size = 2.8) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = lower, ymax = upper), width = 0.1, linewidth = 0.7) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = year_cols) +
  labs(
    title = "Psychological Distress (K6) - National Workforce",
    x = "Quarter",
    y = "Mean K6 score (0-24)",
    colour = "Year",
    ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)
```

As shown in @fig-k6-national, psychological distress appears to rise around Q2, with 2024 remaining elevated into Q3 and 2025 dropping more sharply after Q2. This pattern suggests a possible mid-year pressure point in workforce wellbeing. In practical terms, Q2 may be a useful period for SuperFriend to monitor closely in future ITW waves and to consider earlier prevention or support.

This result should be interpreted as a workforce-level signal rather than individual-level change. The same workers are not followed across waves, so the graph does not show individual workers becoming more distressed in Q2. Instead, it indicates that the average K6 score in the repeated cross-sectional sample appears higher around that period.

The Q2 pattern is important because it answers the first guiding question of the report: **When does risk appear to increase?** If a similar pattern continues in later ITW waves, SuperFriend could use Q2 as a period for closer monitoring, targeted communication or early support. This does not mean that Q2 causes distress. Rather, Q2 may be a useful timing signal that deserves attention in future monitoring.

The confidence intervals are also important. They remind the reader that the plotted means are estimates from survey samples. If intervals are wide or overlapping, differences should be interpreted more cautiously. Including uncertainty supports a more careful interpretation and helps avoid relying only on the visual appearance of the line.

The statistical checks in Appendix A provide additional support for interpreting this pattern as a workforce-level timing signal. However, because the data is repeated cross-sectional, these checks should be read as evidence of differences between survey waves, not as proof that the same workers changed over time.

## Distribution of psychological distress

```{r fig-k6-distribution, fig.cap="Distribution of K6 psychological distress scores by quarter and year. The dashed line shows K6 >= 13, the moderate-to-severe psychological distress threshold. Higher values indicate greater psychological distress", fig.width=7, fig.height=4.4}


# Show the full distribution of K6 scores by quarter and year.
# This helps identify whether high distress is concentrated in a smaller group.
violin_data <- itw_all |>
  filter(!is.na(k6_score), !is.na(quarter), !is.na(year)) |>
  mutate(year = factor(year))

ggplot(
  violin_data,
  aes(x = quarter, y = k6_score, fill = year)
) +
  geom_violin(
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.82),
    alpha = 0.5,
    trim = TRUE,
    colour = NA
  ) +
  geom_boxplot(
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.82),
    width = 0.13,
    outlier.alpha = 0.05,
    colour = "grey25"
  ) +
  geom_hline(
    yintercept = 13,
    linetype = "dashed",
    linewidth = 0.8,
    colour = "#C31861"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = year_cols) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Psychological Distress by Quarter",
    x = "Quarter",
    y = "K6 psychological distress score (0-24)",
    fill = "Year",
    ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)
```

As shown in @fig-k6-distribution, the distribution plot adds detail beyond the average. It shows where most workers are concentrated and whether there is a high-distress tail. This is important because average scores can hide smaller groups of workers who report much higher distress.

The K6 >= 13 threshold highlights workers reporting moderate-to-severe psychological distress. This makes the analysis more actionable because it allows SuperFriend to examine not only the average level of distress, but also whether the higher-risk group appears to change across waves.

This plot also helps explain why both mean trends and distributional views are needed. A mean can increase because the whole distribution shifts upward, or because a smaller group of workers experiences much higher distress. These two situations have different practical implications. A broad upward shift may suggest general workforce pressure, while an expanding high-distress tail may suggest the need for targeted support for a higher-risk group.

For SuperFriend, this distinction matters because workplace action may need to address both population-level and targeted needs. General wellbeing resources may be useful when average distress rises across the workforce. More intensive support may be needed when the group above the K6 threshold becomes larger or more concentrated in particular industries or demographic groups.

The K6 threshold tests in Appendix A provide additional statistical context for the higher-risk group. These tests are useful because they examine whether the proportion of workers above the K6 >= 13 threshold changes across waves, rather than only focusing on changes in average distress.

## Burnout across quarters

```{r fig-burnout-national, fig.cap="Quarterly mean BAT-12 burnout scores with 95% confidence intervals. Lines connect group means for visual clarity only. Higher BAT-12 scores indicate greater burnout", fig.width=7, fig.height=4.3}


# Plot quarterly mean BAT-12 burnout scores to compare burnout with K6 distress.
ggplot(
  burnout_national,
  aes(x = quarter, y = mean, colour = factor(year), group = year)
) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(size = 2.8) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = lower, ymax = upper), width = 0.1, linewidth = 0.7) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = year_cols) +
  labs(
    title = "Burnout (BAT-12) - National Workforce",
    x = "Quarter",
    y = "Mean BAT-12 score (1-5)",
    colour = "Year"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)
```

Burnout appears more stable than psychological distress. This is useful because K6 and BAT-12 capture related but different aspects of worker wellbeing. K6 can reflect sharper psychological distress patterns, while burnout often reflects more accumulated work-related strain.
The burnout scale shows smaller absolute movement than K6, so the pattern should be interpreted as relative stability rather than a large change in burnout.

The relative stability of burnout does not mean burnout is unimportant. Rather, it suggests that burnout may change more slowly than psychological distress and may require ongoing monitoring across several survey waves. This distinction is useful for SuperFriend because it shows why multiple wellbeing measures are needed. If only K6 was analysed, the project may overemphasise sharper short-term distress. If only burnout was analysed, the project may miss more immediate psychological pressure.

Burnout also provides an important comparison point for the Q2 distress signal. If psychological distress moves more sharply while burnout remains more stable, this may suggest that distress is responding to shorter-term pressures, while burnout reflects a more persistent background level of work-related strain. This interpretation remains cautious, but it is useful for planning because acute distress and accumulated burnout may require different types of workplace responses.

# Results: Who appears more affected?

## Why Retail and Transport were selected

Retail and Transport were selected as focus industries because they represent different work environments and provide a useful comparison for examining whether workforce wellbeing signals are industry-specific rather than only national-level patterns.

Retail work is often customer-facing and may involve seasonal demand, roster variability, staffing challenges, customer pressure and exposure to difficult interactions with the public. These conditions can shape how workers experience psychological distress, burnout and financial pressure. Transport work has a different risk profile, often involving operational pressure, scheduling demands, fatigue, time pressure and less flexible working conditions.

Comparing Retail and Transport therefore helps test whether national workforce averages hide different industry risk profiles. This is important because workplace mental health risk is unlikely to be distributed evenly across all industries. A national average can provide a useful overall picture, but it may hide more specific patterns that are important for targeted action.

The purpose of this comparison is not to claim that one industry is universally worse than another. Instead, the aim is to examine whether the pattern of distress, burnout and financial pressure differs across work contexts. If Retail and Transport show different patterns, this suggests that workplace mental health support may need to be tailored by industry context rather than applied in exactly the same way across all sectors.

These explanations are used as contextual interpretation only. The quarterly ITW data cannot prove that a specific event such as Christmas, Easter or a particular week caused distress. However, industry context is still useful for interpreting why some workforce groups may show higher or more variable wellbeing risk across survey waves.

## Retail and Transport: psychological distress

```{r fig-retail-transport-k6, fig.cap="K6 psychological distress in Retail and Transport. Error bars show 95% confidence intervals.", fig.width=7, fig.height=4.4}
# Compare industry-specific mean K6 scores for Retail and Transport.
# These are subgroup summaries, so confidence intervals are important.
ggplot(
  industry_k6,
  aes(x = quarter, y = mean, colour = factor(year), group = year)
) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(size = 2.6) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = lower, ymax = upper), width = 0.1) +
  facet_wrap(~ industry_focus) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = year_cols) +
  labs(
    title = "Psychological Distress: Retail vs Transport",
    x = "Quarter",
    y = "Mean K6 score",
    colour = "Year"
    ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)
```

Retail appears to show more variation in psychological distress than Transport. In particular, Retail shows a clearer rise around Q2, while Transport appears comparatively more stable across quarters.

This suggests that industry-level context matters. However, the result should not be interpreted as proof that Retail work directly caused distress. A more cautious interpretation is that Retail may have a different risk profile and may require more targeted monitoring or support.

This result links directly to the second guiding question: **Who appears more affected?** A national average can hide differences between industries. If Retail shows greater variation, SuperFriend may need to consider whether support messages, employer guidance or workplace interventions should be tailored to customer-facing sectors. This does not mean that Transport is free from risk. Rather, the evidence shown here suggests that the pattern in Transport appears more stable over the quarters examined.

The graph also demonstrates why confidence intervals are important in subgroup analysis. Industry-level estimates may be less precise than national estimates because subgroup sample sizes are usually smaller. The visual comparison should therefore be interpreted together with uncertainty and the threshold tests presented later.

## Retail and Transport: burnout

```{r fig-retail-transport-burnout, fig.cap="BAT-12 burnout in Retail and Transport. Error bars show 95% confidence intervals.", fig.width=7, fig.height=4.4}
# Compare industry-specific BAT-12 burnout patterns for Retail and Transport.
ggplot(
  industry_burnout,
  aes(x = quarter, y = mean, colour = factor(year), group = year)
) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(size = 2.6) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = lower, ymax = upper), width = 0.1) +
  facet_wrap(~ industry_focus) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = year_cols) +
  labs(
    title = "Burnout: Retail vs Transport",
    x = "Quarter",
    y = "Mean BAT-12 score",
    colour = "Year"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)
```

The burnout comparison provides a second view of worker wellbeing. It helps distinguish acute psychological distress from more accumulated work-related strain. Retail appears to show somewhat greater variability, while Transport appears more stable.

This supports the broader finding that worker wellbeing should not be treated as uniform across industries. Different industries may need different forms of support. For example, if Retail shows both distress and burnout variability, interventions may need to address both immediate pressure and longer-term work design or management practices.

Burnout by industry also helps answer a question that can arise from looking only at K6: are industry differences limited to psychological distress, or are they also visible in work-related strain? By including both K6 and BAT-12, the report gives a more balanced view of worker wellbeing. This avoids relying on a single measure and supports a more nuanced interpretation.

## High psychological distress by industry

```{r fig-high-k6-industry, fig.cap="Percentage of workers with K6 >= 13 in Retail and Transport. K6 >= 13 is used as a moderate-to-severe psychological distress threshold.", fig.width=7, fig.height=4.4}

# Calculate the percentage of workers above the K6 >= 13 threshold
# separately for Retail and Transport in each quarter and year.
# This focuses on the higher-risk group rather than only average K6 scores.
high_k6_industry <- itw_all |>
  filter(!is.na(industry_focus), !is.na(k6_high)) |>
  group_by(industry_focus, year, quarter) |>
  summarise(
    high_k6_pct = mean(k6_high == 1, na.rm = TRUE) * 100,
    n = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# Plot the high-distress percentage over quarters by industry.
# Lines connect repeated cross-sectional group summaries for visual clarity only.
ggplot(
  high_k6_industry,
  aes(x = quarter, y = high_k6_pct, colour = factor(year), group = year)
) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(size = 2.6) +
  facet_wrap(~ industry_focus) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = year_cols) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = function(x) paste0(x, "%")) +
  labs(
    title = "Moderate-to-Severe Psychological Distress by Industry",
    x = "Quarter",
    y = "Workers with K6 >= 13",
    colour = "Year"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)
```

The threshold analysis focuses on the higher-risk group rather than only average distress. This is important because a small change in the average may still mask a meaningful change in the proportion of workers above the distress threshold.

```{r threshold-tests-table}
style_report_table(
  industry_threshold_tests |>
    mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 4))),
  caption = "Industry-level K6 >= 13 chi-square and proportion trend tests",
  font_size = 8
)
```

The industry threshold tests provide statistical support for examining whether the proportion of workers above the K6 threshold differs across waves. These results should be interpreted alongside sample size, confidence intervals and the repeated cross-sectional design.

For Retail, the chi-square result suggests that the proportion of workers above the K6 >= 13 threshold varies across waves. However, the trend test is not clearly significant, so the evidence points more to quarter-to-quarter variation than to a simple upward or downward trend. For Transport, the threshold pattern appears less strong. This supports a cautious interpretation: Retail may have a more variable high-distress profile, but the result should be monitored across future waves before making strong claims.

This analysis strengthens the practical value of the industry comparison. Means are useful for understanding overall levels, but threshold proportions are useful for understanding higher-risk groups. If the proportion above K6 >= 13 changes across waves, this may indicate that a larger share of workers is experiencing moderate-to-severe psychological distress. For SuperFriend, this is especially relevant because the higher-risk group may require more targeted support.

## Financial distress by age group

```{r fig-age-financial, fig.cap="Mean financial distress by age group and quarter. Financial distress is measured on a 1-10 scale. Higher values indicate greater financial distress.", fig.width=7.5, fig.height=4.8}
# Create a heatmap of mean financial distress by age group and quarter.
# This shows whether financial pressure is concentrated in particular age groups.
ggplot(
  age_financial,
  aes(x = quarter_full, y = fct_rev(age_group), fill = mean_f10)
) +
  geom_tile(colour = "white", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(mean_f10, 1)), colour = "white", fontface = "bold", size = 3) +
  scale_fill_gradientn(
    colours = c("#00A7C8", "#17B8A6", "#F49E22", "#C31861"),
    name = "Mean financial distress"
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Financial Distress by Age Group",
    x = "Quarter",
    y = "Age group"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))
```

The age-based analysis provides insight into whether financial distress is evenly distributed across the workforce. Younger and working-age groups appear to show higher or more variable financial distress than older groups. This may reflect greater exposure to housing costs, debt, rent, early-career financial commitments or household expenses.

This finding supports targeted support rather than a one-size-fits-all approach. It also shows why the financial distress analysis is useful for SuperFriend's broader mental health work: financial pressure can affect different groups in different ways.

The heatmap is particularly useful because it shows both group differences and time differences at once. If one age group is consistently higher across several quarters, this may suggest a more persistent financial pressure profile. If a group changes sharply in one quarter, it may suggest a temporary or wave-specific pattern. In either case, the graph helps SuperFriend identify where more detailed investigation may be useful.

This result also links financial wellbeing to workforce mental health. Financial distress may not be a workplace condition in the narrow sense, but it can affect workers' stress, attention, wellbeing and need for support. For that reason, financial distress is relevant to SuperFriend's broader goal of supporting mentally healthy workplaces.

## Financial distress by occupation type

```{r fig-worker-financial, fig.cap="Mean financial distress by occupation type. Lines connect repeated cross-sectional group means for visual clarity", fig.width=7, fig.height=4.3}
# Compare mean financial distress for blue-collar and white-collar workers.
# Lines connect repeated cross-sectional group means for visual clarity only.
ggplot(
  worker_financial,
  aes(x = quarter_full, y = mean_f10, colour = worker_type, group = worker_type)
) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(size = 2.6) +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values = c(
      "Blue collar" = "#1E40FF",
      "White collar" = "grey45"
    )
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Financial Distress by Occupation Type",
    x = "Quarter",
    y = "Mean financial distress (1-10)",
    colour = "Worker type"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))
```

Occupation type provides another way to examine whether financial pressure is evenly distributed. Blue collar workers are shown in blue to make the graph easier to interpret. The results suggest that financial distress may differ by type of work, although the size and stability of this difference should be interpreted carefully.

This comparison is useful because job type can shape exposure to financial pressure. For example, workers may differ in income stability, hours, flexibility, bargaining power, access to paid leave and ability to absorb cost changes. The analysis does not directly test these mechanisms, but it shows why occupation type is a relevant grouping variable. The blue-collar group appears consistently higher than the white-collar group, although the size of the gap should be interpreted cautiously because the plot focuses on a narrow financial distress range.

This graph also responds to the practical question of who may need support. If financial distress is higher or more variable among one occupation group, SuperFriend could consider whether financial wellbeing resources should be adapted for different worker segments. The result should be treated as a signal for further analysis rather than a final causal explanation.

# Results: What workplace conditions help contextualise risk?

## SuperFriend workplace domains

```{r fig-domains, fig.cap="SuperFriend workplace domain scores on a 0-100 scale. Higher scores indicate stronger workplace performance or better management.", fig.width=7.5, fig.height=4.8}
# Summarise SuperFriend workplace domains on a 0-100 scale.
# Higher values indicate stronger workplace performance or better management.
domain_plot <- domain_summary |>
  mutate(
    domain = fct_reorder(domain, mean_score, .fun = base::mean, na.rm = TRUE)
  )

ggplot(
  domain_plot,
  aes(x = quarter, y = mean_score, colour = factor(year), group = year)
) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.0) +
  geom_point(size = 2.4) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = lower, ymax = upper), width = 0.1) +
  facet_wrap(~ domain, ncol = 3) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = year_cols) +
  labs(
    title = "SuperFriend Domains - National Workforce",
    subtitle = "0-100 scale: higher scores indicate stronger workplace performance / better management",
    x = "Quarter",
    y = "Mean domain score",
    colour = "Year"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)
```

The SuperFriend domains help translate mental health patterns into workplace levers. Higher domain scores indicate stronger workplace performance or better management. Lower scores suggest areas where workplace support, leadership, work design or capability may need improvement.

The domain results are useful because they show which workplace conditions appear stronger and which may need more attention. Connectedness and Safety can be interpreted as relative strengths, while Capability, Work Design and Leadership provide useful areas for improvement. This helps translate the wellbeing findings into practical workplace levers rather than leaving the analysis only at the level of distress scores.

The domain analysis is important because it connects wellbeing signals to potential areas of organisational action. Psychological distress identifies where workers may be under pressure; domain scores help indicate which workplace conditions may be relevant for improvement.

For example, if distress appears elevated while Leadership or Work Design scores are comparatively lower, this may suggest that workplace practices should be part of the response. The analysis does not prove that domain scores cause distress, but it provides a practical way to connect worker wellbeing outcomes with workplace conditions that organisations can potentially influence.

The 0–100 interpretation is important. These scores should not be read like distress scores. For K6 and financial distress, higher values mean more distress. For domains, higher values mean better workplace conditions. This difference in direction is one reason the key measures table is included early in the report. Without clear definitions, audiences may misinterpret whether a high value is good or bad.

The trend model results in Appendix A provide additional statistical context for the workplace domain measures. The main purpose of @fig-domains, however, is practical interpretation: it helps identify which workplace conditions appear stronger and which may require more attention.

## Psychosocial hazard management

```{r fig-hazards, fig.cap="Psychosocial hazard management scores on a 0-100 scale. Higher scores indicate better management of psychosocial hazards.", fig.width=7.5, fig.height=4.3}
# Summarise psychosocial hazard management scores on a 0-100 scale.
# Higher values indicate better management of psychosocial hazards.
hazard_plot <- hazard_summary |>
  mutate(
    hazard = fct_relevel(hazard, "Management Support", "Recognition", "Workload")
  )

ggplot(
  hazard_plot,
  aes(x = quarter, y = mean_score, colour = factor(year), group = year)
) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.0) +
  geom_point(size = 2.4) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = lower, ymax = upper), width = 0.1) +
  facet_wrap(~ hazard) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = year_cols) +
  labs(
    title = "Psychosocial Hazard Management - National Workforce",
    subtitle = "0-100 scale: higher scores indicate better management of psychosocial hazards",
    x = "Quarter",
    y = "Mean score",
    colour = "Year"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)
```

The psychosocial hazard analysis shows that workplace pressure is not only about workload. Management support and recognition are relational workplace signals that can also matter for worker wellbeing.

If workload remains relatively stable while management support or recognition changes, the practical implication is that organisations should monitor relational and management factors alongside task demands. This interpretation is useful for SuperFriend because it highlights areas where workplace practices may be improved.

This section helps answer the third guiding question: **What workplace context helps contextualise risk?** Psychological distress and burnout describe wellbeing outcomes. Psychosocial hazard management scores help identify workplace conditions that may be relevant to those outcomes. For example, workers may experience pressure not only because of how much work they have, but also because of whether they feel supported, recognised and well managed.

As with the domain scores, higher psychosocial hazard management scores indicate better management. Lower scores suggest areas where stronger workplace action may be useful. This should be clearly distinguished from K6, where higher values indicate greater distress.

The trend model results in Appendix A also provide statistical context for the psychosocial hazard management measures. These results should be interpreted alongside the figure rather than in isolation, because the main value of this section is to connect wellbeing signals with workplace conditions that organisations may be able to influence.

# Results: What economic context helps contextualise risk?

## Prepare economic context data

```{r economic-data-prep}
# ------------------------------------------------------------
# ABS Living Cost Index
# ------------------------------------------------------------

# Read ABS Employee households Living Cost Index data
abs_file <- list.files(
  path = "data",
  pattern = "allgroupsbyhouseholds\\.xlsx$",
  recursive = TRUE,
  full.names = TRUE,
  ignore.case = TRUE
)[1]

if (is.na(abs_file)) {
  stop("Could not find allgroupsbyhouseholds.xlsx in the data folder.")
}

abs_raw <- read_excel(
  abs_file,
  sheet = "Data1",
  col_names = FALSE
) |>
  clean_names()

# Convert ABS date values to quarterly labels so they can be joined to ITW waves
abs_lci <- abs_raw |>
  slice(11:n()) |>
  transmute(
    date_raw = suppressWarnings(as.numeric(x1)),
    employee_lci = suppressWarnings(as.numeric(x3))
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(date_raw), !is.na(employee_lci)) |>
  mutate(
    date = as.Date(date_raw, origin = "1899-12-30"),
    quarter_full = factor(
      paste(format(date, "%Y"), paste0("Q", quarter(date))),
      levels = quarter_order,
      ordered = TRUE
    )
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(quarter_full)) |>
  group_by(quarter_full) |>
  summarise(employee_lci = mean(employee_lci, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

# ------------------------------------------------------------
# RBA cash rate
# ------------------------------------------------------------

# Enter RBA cash rate values by quarter for macroeconomic context
rba_rates <- tibble(
  quarter_full = factor(
    c(
      "2024 Q1", "2024 Q2", "2024 Q3", "2024 Q4",
      "2025 Q1", "2025 Q2", "2025 Q3", "2025 Q4"
    ),
    levels = quarter_order,
    ordered = TRUE
  ),
  cash_rate = c(4.35, 4.35, 4.35, 4.35, 4.10, 3.85, 3.60, 3.60)
)

# Join financial distress summaries with ABS LCI and RBA cash rate data
phase2_dataset <- phase2_financial |>
  left_join(abs_lci, by = "quarter_full") |>
  left_join(rba_rates, by = "quarter_full")

# Calculate simple correlations for descriptive contextual analysis
cor_fin_k6 <- safe_cor(
  phase2_dataset$mean_f10,
  phase2_dataset$mean_k6
)

cor_fin_lci <- safe_cor(
  phase2_dataset$mean_f10,
  phase2_dataset$employee_lci
)

cor_fin_rba <- safe_cor(
  phase2_dataset$mean_f10,
  phase2_dataset$cash_rate
)
```

The economic context dataset combines quarterly financial distress summaries with external economic indicators. The ABS Living Cost Index is used to represent cost-of-living context for employee households. The RBA cash rate is used to represent broader interest-rate conditions. These indicators help interpret worker financial distress, but they should not be treated as proof of causation. The ABS Living Cost Index was used as the external cost-of-living indicator, while the RBA cash rate was used as the external interest-rate indicator (Australian Bureau of Statistics, 2025; Reserve Bank of Australia, 2025).

The economic indicators are included because financial distress does not occur in isolation. Workers experience financial pressure within a broader environment of living costs, interest rates, housing costs, debt repayments and household expenses. External indicators cannot explain individual worker distress on their own, but they provide useful background for interpreting why financial distress may change over time.

## Financial distress and psychological distress

```{r fig-financial-k6, fig.cap="Association between quarterly mean financial distress and quarterly mean psychological distress. Each point represents one quarterly workforce level summary. The line shows association, not causation.", fig.width=7, fig.height=4.4}

# Plot the relationship between quarterly mean financial distress and
# quarterly mean psychological distress.
# Each point represents one quarter, not an individual worker.
ggplot(
  phase2_dataset,
  aes(x = mean_f10, y = mean_k6)
) +
  geom_point(aes(size = n), colour = "#176B3A", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, colour = "black", linetype = "dashed") +
  scale_size_continuous(guide = "none") +
  labs(
    title = "Financial Distress and Psychological Distress",
    x = "Mean financial distress",
    y = "Mean K6 psychological distress"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)
```

As shown in @fig-financial-k6, the relationship between financial and psychological distress shows a positive association. Higher financial distress aligns with higher psychological distress across quarterly summaries. This finding supports the idea that workforce mental health should be interpreted alongside financial wellbeing.

However, the relationship should not be interpreted causally. The analysis shows association at the quarterly workforce-summary level. It does not prove that financial distress directly caused psychological distress.

The correlation results in Appendix A provide statistical support for this positive association, but the relationship remains descriptive. The number of quarterly observations is limited, so the result should be interpreted as useful contextual evidence rather than strong causal or predictive proof.

This result is important because it connects the mental health and financial wellbeing parts of the analysis. Psychological distress, burnout and workplace conditions describe workforce wellbeing outcomes, while financial distress and economic context help describe the broader pressures workers may be experiencing. The positive association between financial and psychological distress supports the argument that worker wellbeing should be understood through both workplace and financial lenses, rather than as separate issues.

For SuperFriend, this has practical significance. Workplace mental health strategies may benefit from recognising that financial wellbeing can be part of the broader wellbeing picture. This does not mean that employers can solve all financial stress, but it does suggest that financial pressure may be relevant when interpreting distress signals in the workforce.

## Living costs and financial distress

```{r fig-lci-financial, fig.cap="Financial distress compared with ABS Employee households Living Cost Index. The LCI is divided by 20 only for visual comparison.", fig.width=7, fig.height=4.4}

# Scale the ABS Living Cost Index so it can be plotted on a similar visual range
# as the financial distress score. This scaling is for visual comparison only.
phase2_lci_plot <- phase2_dataset |>
  mutate(lci_scaled = employee_lci / 20)

# Compare quarterly financial distress with the scaled ABS Living Cost Index.
# The two lines show broad movement over time, not a causal relationship.
ggplot(phase2_lci_plot, aes(x = quarter_full)) +
  geom_line(
    aes(y = mean_f10, group = 1, colour = "Financial distress"),
    linewidth = 1.1
  ) +
  geom_point(
    aes(y = mean_f10, colour = "Financial distress"),
    size = 2.8
  ) +
  geom_line(
    aes(y = lci_scaled, group = 1, colour = "ABS Living Cost Index (scaled /20)"),
    linewidth = 1.1
  ) +
  geom_point(
    aes(y = lci_scaled, colour = "ABS Living Cost Index (scaled /20)"),
    size = 2.8
  ) +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values = c(
      "Financial distress" = "#1E40FF",
      "ABS Living Cost Index (scaled /20)" = "#E31A1C"
    )
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Financial Distress and Living Cost Index Over Time",
    x = "Quarter",
    y = "Financial distress / scaled LCI",
    colour = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))
```

The Living Cost Index increased steadily across the period, while financial distress fluctuated rather than increasing in the same steady way. This is an important result because it suggests that cost-of-living pressure is relevant context, but it does not fully explain financial distress on its own.

One possible interpretation is that workers may adapt to ongoing cost pressures over time, or that other factors such as income, savings, debt, household structure, expectations and employment conditions also shape financial distress. This interpretation should remain cautious because the analysis does not directly test these mechanisms.

The Living Cost Index is scaled only for visual comparison. This means the red line should not be interpreted as being on the same original scale as financial distress. The purpose of the graph is to compare broad movement over time, not to claim that the two measures are numerically equivalent.

This finding is useful because it prevents an overly simple explanation. It would be tempting to assume that financial distress must rise steadily when living costs rise steadily. The data suggests a more complex pattern. Financial distress may respond to living costs, but it may also be influenced by timing, expectations, wage changes, household buffers and adaptation. For SuperFriend, this means that economic context should be considered, but not treated as a single explanation for worker financial pressure.

## RBA cash rate and financial distress

```{r fig-rba-financial, fig.cap="Financial distress compared with the RBA cash rate. Both are shown as raw values because they are on a similar visual scale.", fig.width=7, fig.height=4.4}
# Convert the quarterly financial distress and RBA cash-rate columns
# into long format so both indicators can be plotted in one figure.
# This is used for visual comparison only, not causal modelling.
phase2_rba_long <- phase2_dataset |>
  select(quarter_full, mean_f10, cash_rate) |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(mean_f10, cash_rate),
    names_to = "indicator",
    values_to = "value"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    indicator = case_when(
      indicator == "mean_f10" ~ "Financial distress",
      indicator == "cash_rate" ~ "RBA cash rate (%)"
    )
  )

# Plot financial distress and RBA cash rate over the same quarters.
