```{r fig-rba-financial, fig.cap="Financial distress compared with the RBA cash rate. Both are shown as raw values because they are on a similar visual scale.", fig.width=7, fig.height=4.4}
# The graph shows whether broad movements align over time,
# but it does not prove that interest rates caused financial distress.
ggplot(
  phase2_rba_long,
  aes(x = quarter_full, y = value, colour = indicator, group = indicator)
) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(size = 2.8) +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values = c(
      "Financial distress" = "#1E40FF",
      "RBA cash rate (%)" = "#F49E22"
    )
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Financial Distress and RBA Cash Rate",
    x = "Quarter",
    y = "Score",
    colour = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))
```

The RBA cash rate provides macroeconomic context for household budgets, borrowing costs and debt pressure. As interest rates began to ease in 2025, financial distress also appears to stabilise and decline slightly.

This pattern is useful, but it should be interpreted carefully. It suggests that interest rates may be relevant context for understanding worker financial pressure, but the graph alone does not prove that interest rates caused the observed changes in financial distress.

This graph differs from the Living Cost Index graph because the RBA cash rate and the financial distress score are shown closer to their raw values. The aim is to show whether broad changes in the interest-rate environment align with reported financial distress. Interest rates can affect workers through mortgages, rent, debt repayments and general household financial conditions. However, the analysis is still descriptive and contextual.

For SuperFriend, the practical implication is that economic monitoring may help interpret changes in worker financial wellbeing. If financial distress rises during periods of high or rising interest rates, this may help explain why workers are feeling pressure. However, future analysis would be needed to separate the effect of interest rates from other factors such as wages, inflation, household type and employment conditions.

## Essential costs: housing, utilities and food

```{r fig-essential-costs, fig.cap="Reported ease or difficulty of meeting essential living costs across quarters.", fig.width=7.5, fig.height=4.8}
# Reshape essential cost variables from wide to long format.
# f8_1, f8_2 and f8_3 represent housing, utilities and food.
# This allows the three cost categories to be shown in one faceted plot.
essential_costs <- itw_all |>
  select(quarter_full, f8_1, f8_2, f8_3) |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(f8_1, f8_2, f8_3),
    names_to = "category",
    values_to = "rating"
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(rating)) |>
   # Rename survey item codes so the plot is easier to interpret.
  mutate(
    category = case_when(
      category == "f8_1" ~ "Housing",
      category == "f8_2" ~ "Utilities",
      category == "f8_3" ~ "Food"
    ),
     # Convert numeric response codes into ordered response labels.
    # Lower values indicate more difficulty meeting essential costs.
    rating_label = factor(
      rating,
      levels = 1:5,
      labels = c(
        "Very difficult",
        "Difficult",
        "Neutral",
        "Easy",
        "Very easy"
      )
    )
  ) |>
  group_by(quarter_full, category, rating_label) |>
  summarise(count = n(), .groups = "drop") |>
  group_by(quarter_full, category) |>
  mutate(pct = count / sum(count)) |>
  ungroup()

# Plot the distribution of ease/difficulty ratings for each essential cost.
# Stacked percentages show how responses are distributed within each quarter.
ggplot(
  essential_costs,
  aes(x = quarter_full, y = pct, fill = rating_label)
) +
  geom_col(position = "fill") +
  facet_wrap(~ category) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format()) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "RdYlGn", direction = -1) +
  labs(
    title = "Ease of Meeting Essential Living Costs",
    x = "Quarter",
    y = "Percentage of workers",
    fill = "Ease of payment"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )
```

Essential cost measures make financial distress more concrete. Financial distress is not only an abstract score; it reflects everyday household pressures such as housing, utilities and food. Examining these categories helps explain what financial pressure may look like in practical terms for workers.

This graph is useful because it translates financial distress into practical household categories. A worker may not describe their experience in terms of an index or score, but they may experience financial pressure through difficulty paying rent, mortgage costs, electricity, gas, water or food. This makes the financial wellbeing analysis more meaningful for workplace and policy discussion.

Housing, utilities and food are also useful categories because they represent essential expenses rather than discretionary spending. Difficulty paying these costs may have direct implications for stress, sleep, concentration and overall wellbeing. For SuperFriend, this strengthens the argument that financial distress should be considered as part of the broader workforce wellbeing environment.

# Discussion

## Summary of findings

This report developed and applied a repeatable cross-sectional trend approach for identifying workforce wellbeing signals in ITW survey data. The findings can be summarised through the three guiding questions.

First, psychological distress appears elevated around Q2. This suggests a possible mid-year pressure point that SuperFriend may wish to monitor in future waves. The pattern should not be interpreted as individual-level change, but it may still be useful as a workforce-level early warning signal.

Second, risk appears uneven. Retail, younger workers and occupation groups show different patterns of psychological or financial distress. This supports the idea that workplace support should be targeted rather than generic. A national average may be useful for overall monitoring, but it can hide variation across groups.

Third, workplace and economic context matter. SuperFriend domain scores and psychosocial hazard management indicators provide workplace levers for interpretation. Financial distress, living costs and interest rates provide broader economic context for understanding worker wellbeing.

Taken together, the findings show that workforce wellbeing should be interpreted as a connected system. Psychological distress is one important outcome, but it is better understood when examined alongside burnout, workplace conditions, psychosocial hazard management and financial pressure. This integrated interpretation is more useful for SuperFriend than a set of isolated graphs.

## Link back to the project objective

The project objective was not simply to produce a set of graphs. The objective was to create a repeatable approach that SuperFriend could use to identify meaningful workforce wellbeing signals across ITW waves.

This project contributes by showing how to:

- clean and combine repeated survey waves;
- score mental health, workplace and financial measures consistently;
- compare workforce-level patterns across quarters;
- use statistical checks to avoid overclaiming visual patterns;
- interpret the results in relation to workplace action;
- integrate external economic context without making unsupported causal claims.

The approach is repeatable because it follows a clear workflow: prepare the data, define the measures, summarise by quarter and group, visualise patterns, apply statistical checks, interpret results cautiously and link findings back to practical action. This workflow can be applied to future ITW waves as new data becomes available.

The value of the workflow is that it helps SuperFriend move from reactive reporting to structured monitoring. Instead of only asking what happened in one survey wave, SuperFriend can ask whether patterns are recurring, whether particular groups are more affected, and whether workplace or economic context helps explain the results.

## Practical implications for SuperFriend

A key implication of the analysis is that workplace mental health risk is not evenly distributed. The results suggest that timing, industry, age group, occupation type, workplace conditions and financial pressure all provide useful context. This means that a one-size-fits-all response is unlikely to be enough. A stronger approach is to use repeated survey waves to identify where risk is concentrated and then connect those signals to targeted workplace action.

The findings also support a preventive approach to workplace mental health. Managing psychosocial hazards is essential, but it is only one part of the picture. Workplaces also need to build protective conditions such as strong connectedness, psychological safety, supportive leadership, effective work design and worker capability. These conditions can help reduce harm, support recovery and create environments where workers are more likely to thrive.

The analysis suggests three practical uses for SuperFriend.

First, SuperFriend can use the approach to monitor timing. If the Q2 distress pattern continues in future waves, Q2 may be a useful period for early support, prevention campaigns or additional workplace resources. This could help shift action earlier, before distress becomes more severe.

Second, SuperFriend can use subgroup analysis to target support. Industry, age and occupation differences suggest that risk is not evenly distributed across the workforce. This means support may need to be tailored to the groups most affected. For example, customer-facing industries, younger workers or workers under greater financial pressure may require different messages or resources.

Third, SuperFriend can interpret workplace wellbeing alongside broader economic context. Financial distress, living costs and interest rates do not replace workplace analysis, but they help explain the environment in which workers experience distress. This broader context may be useful for advocacy, employer guidance and future research.

The practical significance of the findings is therefore not limited to one result. The broader contribution is a structured approach for turning repeated survey data into actionable insight. This aligns with the project aim of helping SuperFriend distinguish meaningful signals from normal variation.

# Limitations

This project has several important limitations.

First, the ITW data is repeated cross-sectional rather than longitudinal. This means the analysis tracks workforce-level patterns across survey waves, but it does not follow the same individual workers over time. A longitudinal design would allow stronger conclusions about individual-level change, but it would require repeated participation from the same workers and would likely create high participant burden and attrition. The repeated cross-sectional design is therefore a practical and appropriate approach for monitoring national workforce indicators, but it means the results should be interpreted as workforce-level signals rather than individual trajectories.

Second, the results show patterns and associations, not causal effects. For example, financial distress is associated with psychological distress, but the analysis does not prove that one directly caused the other. Similarly, Retail may show higher or more variable distress, but the analysis does not prove that Retail work itself caused that distress.

Third, some subgroup estimates may have smaller sample sizes. This can increase uncertainty and should be considered when interpreting industry, age or occupation-level patterns. Subgroup results are useful for identifying possible differences, but they should be monitored across future waves before strong conclusions are drawn.

Fourth, quarterly data cannot test specific short-term events such as Christmas week, Easter week or individual workplace incidents. These can be used as contextual explanations, but not as direct causal claims. More detailed time-specific data would be needed to test those explanations directly.

Fifth, external economic indicators such as the ABS Living Cost Index and RBA cash rate are measured at the macro level, while worker distress is measured through survey responses. They provide useful economic context, but they should not be treated as individual-level predictors without further modelling.

Finally, the analysis depends on the consistency and availability of variables across survey waves. If variable names, scoring methods or survey wording change in future waves, the workflow would need to be updated. This is why clear documentation and reproducible code are important outputs of the project.

# Recommendations and next steps

Based on this analysis, the following next steps are recommended.

1. Apply the same repeatable workflow to future ITW waves to test whether the Q2 distress signal persists.
2. Extend industry comparisons beyond Retail and Transport to determine whether other sectors show similar or different patterns.
3. Include uncertainty intervals in key figures wherever possible.
4. Use clear measure definitions early in future reports and presentations to reduce audience confusion.
5. Consider future modelling that tests whether workplace domains, psychosocial hazard management and financial distress jointly explain psychological distress.
6. Apply survey weights consistently across waves if official weighting is required by SuperFriend's methodology.

A further next step would be to develop a monitoring dashboard or automated reporting script that updates the main figures and tables when new ITW data becomes available. This would make the workflow easier for SuperFriend to reuse independently. It would also improve consistency across future reporting cycles.

Another useful extension would be to examine interactions between workplace conditions and financial distress. For example, future analysis could explore whether financial distress is more strongly associated with psychological distress when workplace support is low. This type of analysis could help identify whether workplace conditions buffer or intensify the relationship between financial pressure and mental health.

# Conclusion

This project developed a repeatable approach for analysing ITW survey waves and identifying workforce wellbeing signals across time. The analysis suggests that psychological distress may be elevated around Q2, that wellbeing and financial pressure are not evenly distributed across worker groups, and that workplace conditions and economic context should be considered together when interpreting worker distress.

The findings show that SuperFriend can use repeated cross-sectional ITW data to answer practical questions about when risk appears to increase, who may be more affected, and what workplace or economic context may help explain the patterns. This is useful because it moves the analysis beyond isolated survey results and toward a structured monitoring approach.

The main contribution is not only the individual findings, but the workflow itself. By combining consistent data preparation, visualisation, confidence intervals, statistical checks and cautious interpretation, the approach gives SuperFriend a practical method for monitoring future ITW waves and translating workforce-level signals into targeted workplace action.

Overall, the project supports a preventive approach to workplace mental health. The analysis shows that SuperFriend can use repeated ITW survey waves not only to monitor distress and burnout, but also to identify workplace conditions that may help guide action. The value of the workflow is that it turns repeated survey data into practical signals about when risk appears to increase, who may be more affected, and which workplace or economic factors may help contextualise the pattern.

# My contribution

My contribution was to develop and document a repeatable analytical workflow for SuperFriend's ITW data.

This included:

- Cleaning and harmonising 2024 and 2025 survey files;
- Creating consistent quarterly variables;
- Scoring domain and psychosocial hazard measures;
- Summarising outcomes across quarters, industries and demographic groups;
- Integrating ABS Living Cost Index and RBA cash rate context;
- Conducting independent quarter comparisons, trend models, threshold tests and correlation analysis;
- Designing visualisations;
- Interpreting results with attention to the limitations of repeated cross-sectional data.

This contribution is important because it gives SuperFriend a practical method that can be reused for future survey waves. It also demonstrates the value of business analytics work that combines technical analysis with communication and stakeholder interpretation. The project required not only coding and statistical testing, but also decisions about how to explain uncertainty, how to avoid causal overclaiming, and how to connect results to organisational action.

# Reproducibility

The analysis was completed in R using tidyverse tools for data preparation, visualisation and reproducible reporting (R Core Team, 2025; Wickham et al., 2019).

The source code and project materials for this analysis are available in the Git repository:
<https://github.com/devgur1/ETC5543-Superfriend>

The repository contains the Quarto report file, analysis scripts, figure-generation code, presentation materials and supporting documentation. These materials were organised to make the analysis transparent and reproducible where possible.

The raw ITW survey data is not publicly included because it contains confidential survey responses. Instead, the repository documents the expected data structure and file locations required to reproduce the analysis in an approved environment.

Reproducibility is an important part of this project because the main contribution is not only the findings, but also the repeatable workflow. The same workflow can be rerun when future ITW survey waves become available, allowing SuperFriend to monitor whether the same workforce wellbeing signals continue over time.

# Generative AI acknowledgement

Generative AI tools were used to support report structure, wording, editing and communication. All analysis decisions, code review, interpretation and final content responsibility remain my own. No confidential raw data was entered into generative AI tools.

# References

Australian Bureau of Statistics. (2025). *Living Cost Indexes, Selected Household Types, Australia*. Australian Bureau of Statistics.

Kessler, R. C., Andrews, G., Colpe, L. J., Hiripi, E., Mroczek, D. K., Normand, S.-L. T., Walters, E. E., & Zaslavsky, A. M. (2002). Short screening scales to monitor population prevalences and trends in non-specific psychological distress. *Psychological Medicine, 32*(6), 959–976.

R Core Team. (2025). *R: A language and environment for statistical computing*. R Foundation for Statistical Computing.

Reserve Bank of Australia. (2025). *Cash Rate Target*. Reserve Bank of Australia.

SuperFriend. (2025). *Indicators of a Thriving Workplace survey documentation*.

Wickham, H., Averick, M., Bryan, J., Chang, W., McGowan, L. D., François, R., Grolemund, G., Hayes, A., Henry, L., Hester, J., Kuhn, M., Pedersen, T. L., Miller, E., Bache, S. M., Müller, K., Ooms, J., Robinson, D., Seidel, D. P., Spinu, V., ... Yutani, H. (2019). Welcome to the tidyverse. *Journal of Open Source Software, 4*(43), 1686.

Wickham H, Averick M, Bryan J, Chang W, McGowan LD, François R, Grolemund G, Hayes A, Henry L, Hester J, Kuhn M, Pedersen TL, Miller E, Bache SM, Müller K, Ooms J, Robinson D, Seidel DP, Spinu V, Takahashi K, Vaughan D, Wilke C, Woo K, Yutani H (2019). “Welcome to the tidyverse.” _Journal of Open Source Software_, *4*(43), 1686. doi:10.21105/joss.01686 <https://doi.org/10.21105/joss.01686>.

Wickham H, Miller E, Smith D (2023). _haven: Import and Export 'SPSS', 'Stata' and 'SAS' Files_.
doi:10.32614/CRAN.package.haven <https://doi.org/10.32614/CRAN.package.haven>, R package version 2.5.4,
<https://CRAN.R-project.org/package=haven>.

Firke S (2024). _janitor: Simple Tools for Examining and Cleaning Dirty Data_. doi:10.32614/CRAN.package.janitor <https://doi.org/10.32614/CRAN.package.janitor>, R package version 2.2.1, <https://CRAN.R-project.org/package=janitor>.

Wickham H (2023). _forcats: Tools for Working with Categorical Variables (Factors)_.
doi:10.32614/CRAN.package.forcats <https://doi.org/10.32614/CRAN.package.forcats>, R package version 1.0.0,
<https://CRAN.R-project.org/package=forcats>.

Wickham H, Pedersen T, Seidel D (2025). _scales: Scale Functions for Visualization_.
doi:10.32614/CRAN.package.scales <https://doi.org/10.32614/CRAN.package.scales>, R package version 1.4.0,
<https://CRAN.R-project.org/package=scales>.

Wickham H, Bryan J (2025). _readxl: Read Excel Files_. doi:10.32614/CRAN.package.readxl <https://doi.org/10.32614/CRAN.package.readxl>, R package version 1.4.5, <https://CRAN.R-project.org/package=readxl>.

Garrett Grolemund, Hadley Wickham (2011). Dates and Times Made Easy with lubridate. Journal of Statistical
Software, 40(3), 1-25. URL https://www.jstatsoft.org/v40/i03/.

Robinson D, Hayes A, Couch S, Hvitfeldt E (2026). _broom: Convert Statistical Objects into Tidy Tibbles_.
doi:10.32614/CRAN.package.broom <https://doi.org/10.32614/CRAN.package.broom>, R package version 1.0.12,
<https://CRAN.R-project.org/package=broom>.

Xie Y (2025). _knitr: A General-Purpose Package for Dynamic Report Generation in R_. R package version 1.50,
  <https://yihui.org/knitr/>.

Yihui Xie (2015) Dynamic Documents with R and knitr. 2nd edition. Chapman and Hall/CRC. ISBN 978-1498716963

Yihui Xie (2014) knitr: A Comprehensive Tool for Reproducible Research in R. In Victoria Stodden, Friedrich
Leisch and Roger D. Peng, editors, Implementing Reproducible Computational Research. Chapman and Hall/CRC.
ISBN 978-1466561595

Zhu H (2024). _kableExtra: Construct Complex Table with 'kable' and Pipe Syntax_.
doi:10.32614/CRAN.package.kableExtra <https://doi.org/10.32614/CRAN.package.kableExtra>, R package version
1.4.0, <https://CRAN.R-project.org/package=kableExtra>.
  
# Appendix A: Statistical results

## Linear trend results

```{r appendix-trend-results}
trend_results |>
  select(outcome, estimate, std.error, statistic, p.value) |>
  mutate(
    across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 4))
  ) |>
  style_report_table(caption = "Linear trend model results across quarterly waves", font_size = 7)
```

## K6 threshold tests

```{r appendix-k6-threshold}
style_report_table(
  k6_threshold_tests |>
    mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 4))),
  caption = "National K6 >= 13 chi-square and proportion trend tests",
  font_size = 8
)
```

## Financial distress correlations

```{r appendix-correlations}
correlation_results <- tibble(
  Relationship = c(
    "Financial distress and psychological distress",
    "Financial distress and ABS Employee households LCI",
    "Financial distress and RBA cash rate"
  ),
  Correlation = c(
    unname(cor_fin_k6$estimate),
    unname(cor_fin_lci$estimate),
    unname(cor_fin_rba$estimate)
  ),
  p_value = c(
    cor_fin_k6$p.value,
    cor_fin_lci$p.value,
    cor_fin_rba$p.value
  )
) |>
  mutate(
    Correlation = round(Correlation, 3),
    p_value = round(p_value, 4)
  )

style_report_table(
  correlation_results,
  caption = "Correlation results for financial distress and contextual indicators",
  font_size = 8
)
```

# Appendix B: Independent quarter comparison results

```{r appendix-quarter-tests}
quarter_tests |>
  filter(significant == TRUE) |>
  select(year, outcome, group1, group2, estimate, p.value, p_adj) |>
  mutate(
    estimate = round(estimate, 3),
    p.value = round(p.value, 4),
    p_adj = round(p_adj, 4)
  ) |>
  style_report_table(
    caption = "Significant independent quarter comparisons after Bonferroni adjustment",
    font_size = 6
  )
```



# Appendix C: Computational environment

The analysis was completed using R, Quarto and the tidyverse ecosystem. The main packages used included tidyverse, haven, janitor, readxl, broom, knitr and kableExtra. Full session information is available in the project Git repository to support reproducibility.
